system_description.overview: Minutes AI, LLC operates an AI-powered meeting notetaker that records, transcribes, and summarizes meetings. The platform is cross-platform, supporting iOS, Android, macOS, Windows, and Web, with 600,000+ users, a 4.9 App Store rating, and support for 50+ languages.

products.Minutes AI (AI Meeting Notetaker): Cross-platform AI meeting transcription and summarization; iOS, Android, macOS, Windows, Web; 600,000+ users; 50+ languages supported

infrastructure.cloud_provider: GCP (Google Cloud Platform) - subservice organization (carve-out); GCP has its own SOC 2 report
regions: Not specified by name; multi-availability zone deployment confirmed in GCP
infrastructure.physical_presence: None - no physical servers; operates from working space managed by building management
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ (disaster recovery via 'spinning up multiple servers across all availability zones in GCP')
serverless: Cloud Functions (Google Cloud Functions - shown in network diagram)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: GCP VPC with segmented VPCs and access control lists (ACLs). Network segmentation implemented to separate systems and data based on sensitivity and criticality.
network_architecture.waf: Cloud Armor (Google Cloud Armor - WAF and DDoS protection; shown in network diagram as the first layer of inbound traffic)
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols, and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management. Company uses firewalls and intrusion detection tools configured to prevent unauthorized access and detect external security threats.
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS/IPS deployed via GCP. Firewalls and intrusion detection tools configured to prevent unauthorized access and detect external security threats.
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access via VPN/bastion host (referenced in network description); VPN access confirmed
network_architecture.tls: Encryption technologies used to protect communication and transmission of data over public networks (TLS with certificates from third-party certificate authority)
network_architecture.topology: Internet traffic flows through Google Cloud Armor (WAF/DDoS) → Cloud Load Balancing + Firebase Authentication → Cloud Functions (serverless compute) → Firestore (NoSQL database) → Security Command Center (SIEM/monitoring). GCP VPC with segmented VPCs and ACLs. Remote access via VPN/bastion host.

application_architecture.pattern: Serverless-heavy (Cloud Functions as primary compute; no containers or VMs mentioned)
application_architecture.environments: At minimum: Production, Development/Test (separate environments confirmed by CC8.1.4)

databases: Firestore (Google Cloud Firestore - NoSQL document database; shown in network diagram)
data_storage.backup_frequency: Not explicitly stated (BCDR policy documents recovery procedures; multi-AZ GCP failover confirmed)

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: Firebase Authentication (Google Firebase Auth - shown in network diagram as the IdP layer alongside Cloud Load Balancing); password policy via GCP
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for all user accounts with administrative access to production platform; MFA required for source code tool access (CC8.1.3); MFA required for email access (2FA confirmed)
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination
authentication_access_control.password_policy: GCP password policy enforced: periodic forced changes, password expiry and complexity requirements, account lockout after limited failed attempts

encryption.in_transit: TLS (version not specified; third-party CA certificates)
encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service used to encrypt data at rest (GCP KMS)
encryption.key_management: GCP KMS (cloud provider key management service); access to production environment access keys restricted to authorized individuals

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Git-based source code management tool (MFA required for access; changes logged, time-stamped, and attributed to author)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured to require additional approval for every merge request moving change to production environment
ci_cd_devops.iac: Configuration management tool used for baseline configurations, implementation, and rollback capability (tool not named)
ci_cd_devops.container_orchestration: Not mentioned (serverless Cloud Functions architecture - no containers in network diagram)
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management set up by CTO; monthly patch management reviews; workstations scanned for patch compliance daily; automatic OS updates configured

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not named (monitoring tool generates alerts at predefined thresholds; IT team continuously monitors system capacity and performance)
monitoring_logging.siem: Google Security Command Center (shown in network diagram as the final layer in the data flow - GCP's native SIEM/threat detection service)
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled for admin activities, logon attempts, data deletions, function changes, security configurations, permissions, and roles at application and infrastructure level. Access to log configuration and modification is restricted.
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events; incident management process via ticketing application

third_party_services.purpose: Cloud infrastructure subservice organization - VPC, physical security of data center, serverless compute, database (carve-out)

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and Malware protection software installed and configured on all production servers (continuous scanning, weekly AV definition updates for production servers) and Mac workstations/laptops
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications; management reviews high/critical findings; remediation plan developed for critical and high vulnerabilities
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party; high/critical findings remediated and tracked via incident management process

excluded_criteria: CC6.4 - Physical access controls: GCP responsible for physical security of data center; excluded from scope
A1.2 - Environmental protections/backup/recovery: Minutes AI has no office/operates from working space managed by building management; all environmental security managed by GCP
notable_controls: Google Cloud Armor deployed as WAF/DDoS protection - only company in cohort with explicitly named WAF in network diagram
Firebase Authentication as identity provider - shown in network diagram
Google Security Command Center as SIEM equivalent - shown in network diagram
Cloud Functions (serverless) as primary compute - no container/VM layer
Firestore (NoSQL) as production database - specifically named in network diagram
Quarterly access reviews for all user accounts including privileged accounts
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party
BCDR tested annually with multiple loss scenarios; multi-AZ GCP failover
Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) role explicitly defined (CC2.1.4)
Vendor management: annual reviews of SOC reports from service providers; quarterly risk assessments for critical vendors without SOC reports

infrastructure.rationale: GCP-based with actual named services in network diagram: Cloud Armor (WAF), Cloud Load Balancing, Firebase Auth, Cloud Functions, Firestore, Security Command Center. Multi-AZ deployment confirmed. No multi-region failover. No CDN mentioned. GCP carve-out confirmed with GCP having its own SOC 2. Strongest technical specificity in the cohort for named GCP services.

application_architecture.rationale: Serverless architecture (Cloud Functions) confirmed via network diagram - a strong and specific signal. Separate environments confirmed. Branch protection and code review enforced. No programming languages, frameworks, or specific app patterns disclosed beyond the serverless compute choice. No container orchestration.

data_layer.rationale: Firestore (NoSQL) specifically named in network diagram. Multi-AZ failover via GCP confirmed. Data classification and retention policies exist. No explicit daily backup frequency stated (unlike other companies in cohort). No caching layer or file storage named. Single named database technology.

security_posture.rationale: Best security posture in cohort: Cloud Armor (WAF) explicitly deployed, IDS/IPS via GCP, Security Command Center (SIEM equivalent) named, annual external pen test, monthly vuln scans, MFA across all access, GCP KMS at rest, cybersecurity insurance, quarterly access reviews, antivirus on all endpoints, CISO role defined. Only company with both WAF and named SIEM in network diagram.

devops_maturity.rationale: Branch protection enforced, separate environments, emergency change process, automatic patch management (CTO-managed), BCDR tested annually. CI/CD tool not named, IaC tool not named. No container orchestration (serverless model). Monthly patch reviews. Solid process maturity but limited tooling transparency.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented and reviewed annually. Annual testing with multiple loss scenarios. Multi-AZ failover by spinning up servers across all GCP availability zones. No RTO/RPO disclosed. No explicit daily backup frequency stated. No multi-region failover. Business impact analysis referenced.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Only GCP identified as a subservice organization. No other third-party vendors named. Single cloud provider dependency. Very low vendor diversity disclosure. No backup cloud provider. However, GCP is confirmed as having its own SOC 2 report, which is a positive control.

overall.rationale: Minutes AI has the strongest technical architecture disclosure in this cohort: actual GCP network diagram showing Cloud Armor (WAF), Firebase Auth, Cloud Functions (serverless), Firestore, and Security Command Center (SIEM). This is the only company with an explicit WAF and named SIEM in their architecture. 600,000+ users gives confidence in production-tested scale. SOC 2 Type 1 (not Type 2) limits assurance - design suitability only, no operating effectiveness testing. No backup frequency stated, no RTO/RPO, single named vendor. The GCP-native serverless architecture is modern and appropriate for an AI transcription product. CISO role defined is a positive governance signal.

red_flags: SOC 2 Type 1 only (not Type 2) - design suitability assessed but operating effectiveness not tested; no controls were actually tested over a period
No explicit daily backup frequency stated (all other companies in cohort explicitly confirm daily backups)
yellow_flags: Only one subservice organization (GCP) identified - no other third-party vendors named despite handling audio/video recording and AI transcription (likely uses AI model APIs not disclosed)
No CDN mentioned despite serving a high-volume cross-platform consumer app (600k+ users)
No container orchestration (Cloud Functions only) - limits deployment flexibility
green_flags: Cloud Armor (Google Cloud Armor) deployed as WAF/DDoS protection - only company in this cohort with explicitly named WAF
Security Command Center named in network diagram - only company in cohort with an explicitly named SIEM/threat detection service
Firebase Authentication as named IdP - shown in network diagram
Firestore (NoSQL) specifically named as production database
Cloud Functions (serverless) as primary compute - modern, scalable architecture
600,000+ users with 4.9 App Store rating - evidence of production scale
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party
Monthly automated vulnerability scanning with remediation plan for high/critical findings
MFA enforced across production platform, source code tools, and email
Quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts
Access revocation within one business day of termination
BCDR tested annually with multiple loss scenarios
GCP confirmed to have its own SOC 2 report (subservice organization review)
CISO role explicitly defined with responsibilities for information security policy oversight
Vendor management: quarterly risk assessments for critical vendors without SOC reports
Signed management assertion by Derrick Cai, Co-Founder & CEO
Antivirus on all production servers (weekly definition updates) and Mac workstations
key_observations: Minutes AI has the most technically specific network diagram in this cohort: GCP-native architecture showing Cloud Armor → Cloud Load Balancing + Firebase Auth → Cloud Functions → Firestore → Security Command Center. This is the only company with both a WAF and a SIEM named in their architecture.

The serverless architecture (Cloud Functions + Firestore) is appropriate and modern for an AI transcription service - no container overhead, GCP-managed scaling. This contrasts with the EKS/EC2-based AWS architectures of other companies in the cohort.
600,000+ users suggests production-proven infrastructure at consumer scale, which is a meaningful signal beyond compliance paperwork.

SOC 2 Type 1 (as of July 1, 2025) means Accorp Partners assessed design suitability only - no operating effectiveness testing occurred. This is weaker assurance than Type 2. The company likely plans a Type 2 report in a future period.
Firebase Authentication as the IdP is GCP-native and commonly used for consumer mobile/web apps - consistent with iOS/Android/macOS/Windows/Web cross-platform strategy.

Security Command Center (GCP's threat detection and security analytics service) appearing in the network diagram is a meaningful signal - it is the GCP equivalent of a SIEM and provides continuous vulnerability scanning, threat detection, and compliance monitoring at the infrastructure level.

The absence of an explicit daily backup frequency is notable and differs from all other Accorp Partners reports in this cohort - Firestore has built-in managed backup capabilities but this was not explicitly stated.

The report correctly identifies GCP as having its own SOC 2 - management reviews GCP's SOC report annually to evaluate complementary controls. Quarterly risk assessments for vendors without SOC reports is a mature vendor management practice.
